title: Experimental demonstration of quantum advantage in communication complexity for Euclidean distance problem
authors: Verena Yacoub, Niraj Kumar, Iordanis Kerenidis, Eleni Diamanti
abstract: When considering the complexity of communication protocols, the aim is to perform a certain task with the minimum amount of communication resources, such as time and transmitted information. The use of quantum states may lead to an exponential advantage in the use of such resources. Here, we are interested in the task of calculating the Euclidean distance between two vectors representing real data sets. It has been previously shown that it is possible to obtain an advantage for this task based on quantum fingerprinting. This protocol is defined in the simultaneous message passing model of communication complexity, where the two parties do not communicate with each other but send data to a third party, and exploits practical fingerprints generated using trains of coherent state pulses instead of highly entangled qubit states that are hard to generate for large input sizes needed to demonstrate an exponential advantage. We perform a proof-of-principle experimental demonstration of the Euclidean distance protocol using amplitude modulation techniques for encoding non-binary data sets and high-performance superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors required to increase the accessible input size. We show a quantum advantage in transmitted information surpassing the best classical protocol for an input size of $10^8$, for diverse types of data sets, including those corresponding to real grayscale images, and with reasonable precision and error bounds. Our results highlight the potential of quantum communication complexity for use in a broad set of applications.
summary: This work experimentally proves a quantum advantage for calculating the Euclidean distance in a communication complexity setting using photonic quantum states. By encoding real-valued data into coherent pulse amplitudes, the authors achieved a quantum advantage factor of $\approx 1.48$ for $n=10^8$ compared to classical bounds. The demonstration validates the potential of quantum communication protocols for practical data processing tasks.
main_problem: The paper experimentally demonstrates a quantum advantage in communication complexity for calculating the Euclidean distance between two high-dimensional real vectors.
main_result: A quantum advantage was shown for an input size of $10^8$, where the quantum protocol achieved a transmitted information rate significantly surpassing the best known classical protocol bound.
method: The protocol uses quantum fingerprinting adapted for real-valued vectors, implemented via weak coherent pulse trains interfering on a beam splitter.
model_or_system: The experiment utilizes an interferometric setup with superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) to encode non-binary data in the amplitudes of coherent states.
key_observables: Estimated Euclidean distance ($	ilde{E}$), quantum transmitted information ($I_Q$), and the quantum advantage factor ($A_{best}$).
important_parameters:
Input size ($n$) up to $10^8$; mean photon number ($\mu$) tuned in the range of $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$; visibility ($
u$) and detector quantum efficiency ($\eta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the weak-pulse approximation and the use of the private-coin simultaneous message passing (SMP) model.
figures_summary: Figures show histograms confirming the estimated Euclidean distances for random and grayscale image inputs, verifying the results within a specified error bound.
paper_structure: The paper builds from the theoretical background of communication complexity and quantum fingerprinting, derives the necessary bounds (Chernoff/Gaussian), details the experimental implementation using coherent states, and concludes with the quantitative demonstration of the quantum advantage.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, large-scale experimental realization of a quantum advantage in a fundamental information-theoretic task (communication complexity) using photonic systems, which is highly relevant to quantum communication research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: General scheme of the Euclidean distance protocol in the simultaneous message passing model. The real valued information vector is encoded on the amplitudes of the coherent pulses constituting the fingerprints.

FIG. 2: Log-log plot of the transmitted information as a function of input size n for solving the ED problem with additive accuracy ϵ = 0.1 and failure probability δ ≤10−6. We compare the ideal (V = 1, pd ≈10−14) and realistic (V = 0.9, pd ≈10−7) ED protocol against the classical lower bound and the best known classical protocol for Equality. Experimental points are shown with black symbols. Quantification of the the quantum advantage is in Section III and the detailed calculations in Appendix C.
FIG. 3: Experimental setup for the implementation of the Euclidean distance protocol.
FIG. 4: Grayscale images used as Alice’s (upper row) and Bob’s (lower row) input data sets in the real-data experiment.
FIG. 5: Distributions of the estimated Euclidean distance over repeated protocol runs. The shaded pink region indicates the ±0.1 error bound.
